data.title: Best Buy: Signs Of Progress, But Still No Clear Bull Case - Hold
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Best Buy (BBY) delivered positive comparable sales growth in the last two quarters, and I see this as a big step in the right direction. However, I am concerned about the company’s ability to build on this improvement over the next four quarters. The recent recovery in sales has relied heavily on two product categories, with the launch of the Nintendo Switch 2 playing a major role. This raises the risk that comparable sales growth could start slowing down again starting in Q2 FY27 as we lap the Switch 2 launch. I need to see strength in sales across a broader set of categories to become a bull on BBY.

However, there are a few things that I like about the stock that would keep it in my portfolio:

  1. The stock has a high free cash flow (FCF) yield of 9%, and the company returns most of it back to shareholders in the form of dividends (dividend yield of 4.7%) and share buybacks.
  2. Valuation multiples are in line with historical trends, which leaves significant room for the share price to expand when the sentiment turns bullish.
  3. The omnichannel pivot makes Best Buy a strategic link between manufacturers and consumers. The stores enable secure pickups for high value online purchases (the Switch 2 is a very good example) and serve as experiential marketing touchpoints for brands through shop-in-shop concepts. Management also highlighted that stores are connecting with the Gen Z shoppers, which is an encouraging sign.

I remain a fan of Best Buy and see merits in owning the stock. The stock might have a good bounce following Q3 FY26 results that beat expectations. However, the Q4 FY26 comparable sales guide of -1% to 1% is unlikely to give the stock much room to run.

Comparable Sales Have Turned Positive

Comparable sales have turned positive over the last two quarters, with BBY posting a 2.7% YoY increase in comparable sales growth in Q3 FY26. This reverses a multi-year slide from the pandemic peak. Figure 1 shows how this trend has been gradually improving over the last 3 years. Ideally, this is the kind of trend that you look for when you are searching for signs of a change in outlook. Thus, it is important to look at the components of this growth to assess whether this strength is sustainable.

Figure 1 Comparable Sales Growth by Quarter

In the next part of my analysis, I focus on the comparable sales growth by categories for the domestic segment. The domestic segment accounts for 90% of sales, and this makes it the relevant benchmark to look at. Figure 2 shows the components of domestic comparable sales for the last 3 quarters. There are 3 main takeaways here from Figure 2. First, Computing and Mobile Phones (~45% of sales) have been doing really well. This was boosted by customers upgrading to Windows 11. Second, the Entertainment segment had very high growth in Q2 and Q3 on the back of the launch of the Nintendo Switch 2. Third, other segments were still experiencing steep and consistent pressure on sales. Thus, there is some good underlying strength, but some weak parts remain.

Figure 2 Domestic Comparable Sales Growth by Category

Margins Have Been Relatively Stable

The gross profit margin (GPM) has come under pressure in the last two quarters, but the company has been able to offset most of this pressure. The GPM is being squeezed by growth in lower-margin categories as well as increased product costs, including tariffs. However, the effect on operating profit margin has been muted, as the company has been reducing SG&A as a percentage of sales. Management deserves credit for protecting the adjusted operating profit margin. With costs being well contained, the key focus for the stock should be on sales recovery.

Figure 3 YoY Change in Margins

Cash Returns to Shareholders

BBY is a strong free cash flow (FCF) generator. The company produced $1.5 billion in FCF over the last twelve months which translates to an FCF yield of 9%. BBY returns most of this FCF to shareholders in the form of dividends and share buybacks. Currently, BBY is paying a quarterly dividend of $0.95 per share that results in an annual dividend yield of 4.7%. While there is some scope to increase buybacks, I do not expect the company to move in that direction at the current share price. Management has shown discipline in optimizing shareholder returns without compromising balance sheet health.

Figure 4 Cash Flow Bridge

Valuation & Outlook

My forecasts for FY26 are broadly in line with management guidance, as I do not see any compelling reason to deviate from their outlook. My sales forecast for FY26 matches the higher end of the range for the sales guidance. Also, I do not expect to see any major changes in the operating profit margin outlook. Thus, my expectations are consistent with management guidance, with an adjusted operating profit margin of 4.27% and an adjusted diluted EPS of $6.25. It is worth noting that management has guided to a muted sales outlook for Q4 FY2026, which suggests that it does not expect the strength from Q2 and Q3 to carry into Q4.

From a valuation standpoint, the stock trades in line with its historical 5-year average. However, BBY’s EV/Sales of 0.47x sits well below the sector average of 1.3x. I believe that the stock has the ability to see its multiples expand if we get confirmation of continued sales momentum over the next three quarters. I would not call BBY a bargain here, but it is far from expensive at current levels.

Figure 5: FY2026 Forecasts and Implied Multiples

Key Risks

BBY is an electronic retailer and is exposed to a multitude of risks that relate to consumption. BBY sells several high-ticket items for which consumers can defer purchases for during weak economic times. The company also faces stiff competition from large retailers such as Amazon and Walmart. The latest improvement in sales was sparked by upgrades to Windows 11 and the Nintendo Switch 2, which may be one-off boosts if no similar product cycles come into play in the future.

Final Thoughts

BBY had a good but not great quarter in Q3 FY2026. The company was able to build on the strength seen in Q2 but stopped short of including this momentum in its Q4 outlook. Regardless, we appear to be very close to an inflection point in sales growth, which is the catalyst that the stock needs. Valuation is not demanding, and the stock offers a decent FCF yield of 9%. I prefer to wait for more clarity on the sales trajectory before turning bullish on the stock. I am assigning a Hold rating on the stock.

data.title: Coinbase: The Crypto Selloff Has Created An Opportunity
data.description:
During the middle of the year, I reiterated my buy rating for Coinbase Global, Inc. (NASDAQ:COIN). Despite continuing to rally for some time after my update, fortunes for the stock have reversed. Recently, the stock has experienced significant weakness, but that really isn't too surprising with Bitcoin (BTC-USD) prices under major pressure. The company reported earnings at the end of October, and today, we'll be conducting an analysis to see whether current levels represent a buying opportunity.

In the discussion below, it is shown that Q3 was a strong quarter. While crypto prices may weigh on Q4 transaction revenues, with strong internals and robust monetization, I expect this business to rebound quickly as headwinds dissipate. For their subscription and services business, Q3 was also strong as stablecoin momentum is solid. Despite Q4 guidance hinting at a slowdown in growth for this segment, long-term government policy should provide continued tailwinds. Therefore, I view the recent valuation contraction as a buying opportunity and thus reiterate my buy rating on the stock.

Financial Overview

Revenues

Before diving deeper into their segment results, let's take a broad view of their Q3 earnings results. For the quarter, Coinbase reported net revenues of $1.793 billion, and you can calculate from the above figures that the YoY growth rate is a very respectable 58.81%. In the previous quarter, growth was reported to be just 2.90% for net revenues, and so it is quite obvious that their business saw great activity in the quarter.

In GAAP terms, total revenue was $1.8687 billion, and that includes corporate interest and other income. This was able to beat analyst expectations by $60.37 million to show a good degree of outperformance. You can also see above that their Subscription and Services revenue surpassed their own guidance. As a whole, prior to the November crypto selloff, Coinbase's business was booming, and therefore, the recent weakness in the stock is likely driven by exogenous factors. With the company so strong before the crypto weakness, they should be well positioned to bounce back from any Q4 weakness when the crypto market stabilizes and rebounds.

Expense Management

In terms of key operating expenses, you can also see above that they were well within their previously provided ranges. Transaction expenses as a percentage of revenues were 14%, and that represents an improvement from Q2's 17% and from the prior year period's 15%, demonstrating solid efficiency. For Sales and Marketing, expenses increased 57.95%, and for General and Administrative, there was an increase of just 26.63%. Both of these increases were less than the net revenue growth rate, and so as a whole, corporate efficiency was also robust. As for Technology and Development, they spent just 14.10% more than they did in the year ago quarter. While that's quite beneficial for near-term profitability, this may indicate they are underspending for long-term growth.

Profits Rocket

Coinbase's net income and EPS are quite erratic at times, and so a likely better measure of their core profitability is adjusted EBITDA. As shown above, they reported $801 million in Q3, and that represents a 78.40% increase YoY. With topline growth being strong and expense management being overall impressive, this shouldn't be a major surprise. Back in the previous quarter, adjusted EBITDA actually declined by 14.09%, and so Q3 was a very promising quarter on many measures. Now, let's take a look at their two core segments.

Segment Results

Transaction Revenue

Transactions are still the company's largest source of revenue currently, and so it is understandable why investors are spooked by the crypto selloff. Total trading volumes were up 59.46% YoY but revenues for the segment increased by a whopping 82.76%, and so it seems that their monetization of the volume boom was excellent. As shown above, Bitcoin is still dominant in both volume and revenues and so with this cryptocurrency making headlines for continued weakness, again investors are unsurprisingly unnerved. That said, this strong monetization shows that the internals of the business are robust and positions them well for the long run when the crypto market recovers.

Subscription and Services Revenue

Coinbase's Subscription and Services segment is a core part of the business, with stablecoins gaining much momentum. Overall, revenues were up 34.27% to $746.7 million, and while this growth rate trails Transactions' revenue increase, it still indicates solid activity. Firstly, the company stated that they "believe Coinbase continues to be the best place to use stablecoins." As you can see above, they earn a lot more revenue per unit of market cap for USDC in Coinbase products than they do for off-platform USDC. Therefore, it would make great sense for them to create a great environment to attract users and retain them on their platform.

At the end of the year-ago quarter, they had a total of $36 billion in USDC market cap, with $8.1 billion being on-platform. Therefore, total market cap has grown by 88.89%, while on-platform balance has grown by 85.19%. This could be slightly disappointing, as it shows growth in off-platform USDC is outperforming, which in turn indicates that the company can likely still do better in making Coinbase an attractive platform to use stablecoins.

Guidance A Bit Shaky

The company provided guidance for Q4 for select metrics. Note how they exclude the outlook for transaction revenue, as there is volatility in the results due to crypto price fluctuations.

For their Subscription and Services revenues, the midpoint of the above range implies a growth rate of 17% YoY. That would be quite a deceleration from the Q3 figure that we just discussed, and so perhaps the momentum of stablecoins is expected to plateau somewhat.

For transaction expenses, they expect it to remain in the mid-teens as a percentage of net revenues, and so there won't be any major changes from Q3. As for technology & development + general & administrative expenses, they are projecting a 30% increase. While an increase in development spending isn't a bad thing at all, this quite outsized increase may be from G&A, and if that's the case, profitability is implied to be under a bit of pressure, especially if transaction revenues come in soft. For sales and marketing expenses, the midpoint of the above range represents a 17% increase, far less than in Q3, and so expense management is likely to be mixed as a whole in the near term.

Overall, guidance is a bit shaky, and while an outlook for transactions isn't given, it can be inferred that there will be weakness given what we saw in crypto markets in November.

Valuation Pullback Is An Opportunity

Coinbase's forward P/E ratio has seen quite a steep contraction recently, and that could be a buying opportunity even if the company faces some headwinds and challenges.

Firstly, the elephant in the room is the crypto selloff. It was stated earlier that the company still relies a lot on transactions and on Bitcoin in particular. Crypto prices obviously have a direct impact on their revenues, but I would say there is too much correlation between Coinbase stock and these prices. The internals of their business remain unchanged, and monetization of trade volumes was shown to be strong. Once crypto rebounds, the business will follow in a strong manner, and so I would say investors in Coinbase are overly reactive to volatility in crypto prices.

Secondly, while guidance above suggests some plateauing of stablecoin momentum, I would say the long term remains bright for this space given trends in Government policy. Therefore, I view the recent valuation contraction in Coinbase as a buying opportunity.

Final Thoughts

Q3 was a strong quarter for Coinbase and while a sudden souring of the environment may cause weaker than desired Q4 results, the company clearly remains well positioned for the future of crypto and the future of stablecoins. Good monetization and platform attractiveness are internal strengths that crypto price headwinds can't really shake, and so I view the recent selloff in the stock as a prime opportunity to pick up this leader. As a result, I'm reiterating my buy rating on the stock.

data.title: Investors Thankful For...
data.description:
Amid the ups and downs of the past year, including the "tariff crash" in April, the US stock market has still posted a double-digit total return since last Thanksgiving.

Below is a look at the performance of various asset classes since the close before last Thanksgiving using key ETFs traded on US exchanges.

The tech-heavy Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) and S&P 500 Growth (IVW) have been the best performers of the domestic index ETFs shown, with gains of more than 20%.

On the flip side, mid-caps (IJH, IJJ, IJS, IJK) and small-caps (IWM, IJT, IJS) have been either flat or down over the last year.

Looking at US sector ETFs, there has been quite a bit of divergence, with Consumer Staples (XLP, Energy (XLE), Materials (XLB), and Real Estate (XLRE) all in the red since last Thanksgiving, while Technology (XLK), Communication Services (XLC), and Utilities (XLU) are all up 10%+.

Aside from Australia (EWA), Argentina (ARGT), and India (INDA), the country ETFs listed in our matrix are all up 20%+ over the last year, so investors that decided to gain more exposure outside of the US have a lot to be thankful for.

The same goes for investors long the gold (GLD) and silver (SLV) ETFs. These two precious metal ETFs are up the most in our entire matrix!

On the flip side, the iShares Bitcoin ETF (IBIT) has been the worst performer in the matrix since last Thanksgiving, with a decline of 9.96%. Just a month or so ago, IBIT owners had a lot to be thankful for, but after a 30% drawdown from highs, now it's "not so much."

Speaking of being thankful, anyone who managed to go long the names below a year ago should probably be offering to pay for this year's Thanksgiving turkey.

The stocks shown are Russell 3000 names that are up more than 250% since last Thanksgiving, of which there are thirty.

If you're just interested in the largest, most well-known stocks, below is a list of names with market caps of $50+ billion that have done the best over the last year.

There are eight that have doubled: Robinhood (HOOD), Western Digital (WDC), Palantir (PLTR), Broadcom (AVGO), Micron (MU), Warner Bros. (WBD), Lam Research (LRCX), and Newmont (NEM).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Dividend Champion, Contender, And Challenger Highlights: Week Of November 30
data.description:
Introduction

The Dividend Champions list is a monthly compilation of companies that have consistently increased their annual dividend payouts. However, since this list is only produced once per month, the data in it can quickly get out of date. Furthermore, with around 700 companies on the list, the sheer amount of data can quickly become overwhelming. In this weekly series, I highlight recent and upcoming dividend-related activity for dividend stocks on the Dividend Champions list.

In the data presented below, Yield is forward annualized, and Years reflects the up-to-date streak, including dividends declared since the last edition of the Dividend Champions list.

data.title: Grupo Financiero Galicia: Not The Best Option For Betting On Argentine Growth
data.description:
Grupo Financiero Galicia (GGAL) is a financial holding company operating in Argentina. Within the conglomerate is one of the country's largest private banks (Banco de Galicia y Buenos Aires), as well as other credit card companies (Naranja X), stockbrokers (Inviu), and other businesses that contribute to the overall ecosystem.

Following the midterm elections for the National Congress, the company has seen a rapid recovery from the downward trend it exhibited over the past year. After attacks from the opposition, primarily Kirchnerism, against the government of Javier Milei and especially against its fiscal surplus, Argentine markets entered a period of high volatility.

Following the resounding victory of President Milei's party and the prospect of the country undertaking a series of reforms to boost economic growth, GGAL's stock price rebounded strongly. However, as I intend to demonstrate below, the financial sector is not, in my view, one of the primary vehicles for leveraging the potential development of Argentina's productive forces under the anticipated reforms. While I believe GGAL will benefit from the expected economic growth, it will not be among the top gainers, and therefore I consider it a HOLD.

Thesis

As I mentioned previously, GGAL is a holding company dedicated to the financial industry that operates in Argentina through its participation as a bank and its credit card companies, virtual wallets, and stockbrokers.

As I mentioned before, the opposition's advance, particularly with the implementation of a series of laws favoring increased pensions and spending on both health and education, jeopardized the national government's fiscal surplus. This being one of the main economic anchors, and with the lack of representation of the party led by Milei becoming evident at the previous congress, volatility in the Argentine markets skyrocketed. With a generalized drop in bond prices, increased pressure on the FX market, and a sharp rise in the local interest rate in an attempt by the Central Bank (BCRA) to prevent runaway inflation, real economic activity suffered a slowdown in its recovery. Undoubtedly, the financial sector was severely affected by this situation.

During the second half of 2024 and the beginning of 2025, the stability of the country's macroeconomic variables had led to a recovery in both Argentina's GDP and industrial production. These achievements were undermined as the opposition, through Congress, attacked the country's fiscal surplus. Following their resounding victory in October, Milei's party secured the largest minority in Congress. Given the alliances the government may forge with representatives loyal to Macri and the governors, the likelihood of passing what the government calls "second-generation" reforms increases considerably. These reforms include the budget law, labor law reform, and tax reform aimed at lowering taxes.

However, it is important to consider two facts that, from my perspective, are extremely relevant to the sector. First, the financial sector saw an increase in its profits in a context of high spreads between deposit and lending rates. Second, the improvement in the value of government bonds held by banks also brought extraordinary benefits to the financial system as a whole. Finally, it is worth noting that these measures are geared more towards a recovery of the real economy, which, in relative terms, is much further behind than the financial industry, which reacted more quickly.

Regarding monetary policy, which has the greatest impact on the financial system, Milei's rise to the presidency presupposed a sharp contraction of the monetary base in order to control inflation. On the other hand, the fiscal surplus meant the government had less need for financing, which disrupted a particular feature of the Argentine financial system: the national government itself was the main agent absorbing banks' lending capacity. In this scenario, banks began to restructure themselves, channeling liquidity back into loans to the private sector. The main obstacle was the risk of default from an industry still recovering, reflected in high-interest rates.

This high spread between lending and deposit rates generated a high ROE for Argentine banks, far exceeding that of banks in other countries in the region, let alone those in the United States. However, as can be seen below, as the disinflationary trend continues and the economy continues to recover, the spread is expected to narrow. The question then arises: where might new business opportunities arise for banks in Argentina? The answer lies in quantity. Currently, as a result of very high inflation, the country has experienced a demonetization of the economy.

As can be seen below, even when compared to other economies in the region, the Argentine financial system is extremely small relative to the size of its GDP. Bank deposits represent 18% of GDP, compared to 71% in Brazil or 63% in Chile. Regarding loans, the situation is even more pronounced, as in Argentina they represent only 10% of GDP, compared to countries like Brazil at 69% and even smaller economies like Peru at 47%. This highlights the potential for growth in the financial sector.

However, it is necessary to understand Argentina's idiosyncrasies before assuming that the country will experience a strong monetization of the economy in the short term. While it is true that the Milei administration has implemented a series of improvements aimed at facilitating this process—lower inflation being one of the key factors—the reality is that the inflationary process and the high demand for dollars by economic agents in a context of restricted FX markets (currency controls) have generated significant distrust in the Argentine peso (ARS).

On the other hand, to expect a remonetization of the economy that would boost deposits and loans to the private sector, a strong recovery of the real economy would first be necessary. While the reforms that the Mieli government hopes to implement by the beginning of 2026 are in line with the restructuring and modernization the country needs, the reality is that the vast majority of them target that part of the real economy. Furthermore, the vast majority of confirmed foreign investments in the country are related to the mining, oil and gas, and infrastructure sectors.

I would now like to discuss the bank's structure and briefly compare it with its main competitors in both the public sector (Banco Nación and Banco de la Provincia de Buenos Aires) and the private sector (Banco Santander Argentina, BBVA Argentina, Banco Macro, and ICBC Argentina). In terms of market share, GGAL is among the leading financial institutions operating in the country, second only to Banco de la Nación Argentina (BNA). This position holds true both in terms of deposits and the value of loans granted.

In terms of the number of credit cards issued, Banco Galicia ranks first. It's important to note that in the chart below (because the complete merger of the two entities has not yet been finalized), the bank reports separately the figures for Banco Galicia itself and Galicia Mas (formerly HSBC Argentina). Combining these figures shows that GGAL surpasses Santander in first place.

It is therefore evident that GGAL is one of the leading companies in the Argentine financial sector. Given the potential remonetization of the economy, its structure and market share undoubtedly make it a very attractive candidate. However, I believe it is important to emphasize that the remonetization process, which could improve the bank's position, will only be seen in the Argentine economy in the medium term, with an initial boost coming from the real economy. That's why I'm considering giving GGAL a HOLD rating.

3Q2025 Results

In this section, I would like to mention the bank's latest published results. As you can see, GGAL reported losses, primarily due to the sharp increase in market volatility, a rise in the benchmark interest rate from an average of 35% annually to nearly 80%, and an increase in the reserve requirement ratio by the Central Bank of Argentina (BCRA).

It is within this adverse context of a deepening contractionary monetary policy that Argentine banks posted weak results in Q3 2025. As I mentioned, this situation ended in late October after the elections, and a recovery in Q4 2025 is highly likely. However, the theory that remonetization of the Argentine economy will be delayed is gaining traction, and in this context, companies in the real economy become more relevant.

That said, and before presenting the results, it's important to note that they are expressed in ARS, with an FX exchange rate of ARS 1425 per dollar. Now, I'd like to move on to the results. Galicia's net operating income was ARS 1,236 billion. This represents a 23% decrease compared to ARS 1,613 billion. Regarding the net operating result for the period, it was a negative ARS 87,700 million (equivalent to a loss of $61.5 million). This is a significant drop compared to the ARS 183 billion of Q2 2025.

This represents a return on equity (ROE) of -4.7% for the period, bringing the year-to-date total to 4.7%, a significant decline from the more than 9% recorded in the previous quarter. Another important point to highlight is the slight deterioration in the non-performing loan ratio, which increased from 3.3% in Q2 2025 to 3.6% in the last quarter. Similarly, credit risk rose from 10.9% to 12.2% quarter-over-quarter. This marked deterioration is primarily due to the effects of the sharp increase in the real interest rate resulting from the contractionary monetary policy.

Valuation

For this segment, I set out to value the company based on a multiples comparison. When choosing comparable companies, I decided not to limit myself to other Argentine banks to avoid biasing the analysis. Therefore, to compile the sample, I selected banks from Brazil and Mexico, the leading Latin American economies, which share certain similarities with Argentina. Using Chilean banks, given the strong monetization, formality, and depth of their capital markets, wouldn't have made much sense in my view. Nor should they have used banks from countries with smaller economies, or Asian banks where the development of industry and the economic structure of the countries would have been very different.

To develop the analysis, I divided the data used into two parts. First, the multiples I took from each of the companies, which helped me arrive at a target price. Second, a series of economic and financial indicators to characterize GGAL within the companies I selected as comparables and to see if it is below or above the sample average.

Regarding the ratios segment, to characterize the companies in the industry, I used the P/E (Forward-Width) ratio, the P/S ratio, and finally, the P/BV ratio, which I consider the most representative. Based on the values ​​of each ratio for the different companies, I calculated a sample ratio that emerged as a market-cap-weighted average. From this value, I calculated a target price for GGAL using each of the ratios. Finally, using the weighting factors shown in the table, I arrived at a target price. As can be seen in the table, using these assumptions, the valuation of GGAL is practically equal to the share price. This indicates that the market has already priced in the short-term growth that the financial industry in general, and GGAL in particular, may experience following the victory of Milei's party in the parliamentary elections.

Risks

To conclude, I would like to mention some of the main risks we can expect in the short term. Primarily regarding the Argentine financial sector, and particularly concerning GGAL, the volatility of macroeconomic variables will be a key factor. To the extent that the government is unable to consolidate alliances that would allow it to implement reforms and loses political capital, the opposition may return to its aggressive agenda against Milei's presidency. In this sense, increased volatility could again force the government to raise interest rates, put pressure on the foreign exchange market, and prompt the Central Bank to take measures regarding reserve requirements. While, from my perspective, this is unlikely given the government's consolidated victory, the speed with which Milei's party moves in Congress to approve some of the reforms in the short term and demonstrate its proactive stance will be important.

data.title: PGHY: International High-Yield Dividends
data.description:
Thesis

We last covered the Invesco Global ex-US High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (PGHY) over two years ago on the Seeking Alpha platform. At the time, we went through the mandate change for the fund and its composition.

In today's article we are going to revisit this name and its performance, its current portfolio composition, and its outlook given the macro cycle.

Fund mandate and current composition

Let us start off by looking again at the current fund objective as per its website:

The Invesco Global ex-US High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (FUND) is based on the ICE USD Global High Yield Excluding US Issuers Constrained Index (Index). The Fund generally will invest at least 80% of its total assets in the components that comprise the Index, all of which are denominated in US dollars. The Index is composed of U.S. dollar denominated below investment grade corporate debt publicly issued in the U.S. domestic and Eurobond markets by non-U.S. issuers.

The ETF therefore holds international high yield bonds, specifically excluding U.S. issuers. Its current geographic reach is as follows:

Canada represents the largest exposure at 12.3%, followed by Brazil at 12% and Turkey at 7.2%. Please note the countries of origin for the issuers represent a blend of developed markets and emerging markets. From an individual issuer standpoint, the components for the ETF shape up as follows:

Industrial & Commercial Bank of China is a top exposure at 1.4% of the fund, followed by Samarco Mineracao at 1.09%.

From a ratings standpoint, the ETF is overweight BB-rated credits:

BB names represent 62% of the fund, followed by single-B names at 22% and BBB credits at 10.9%. Other fund analytics are as follows:

  • AUM: $0.18 bil
  • Expense ratio: 0.35%
  • Duration: 3.3 years
  • Holdings: 566 names
  • Current yield: 7%

Performance is in line with U.S. peers

Let us take a look at the ETF's performance since we last covered it:

  • 2023: 10.1% (versus 12.4% for JNK)
  • 2024: 8.3% (versus 7.7% for JNK)
  • 2025: 7.9% (versus 8% for JNK)

We are comparing the name's performance versus the U.S. version of high yield, namely the State Street® SPDR® Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK). In fact, not only did they post similar results, but they are also very correlated:

We have used a total return graph above in order to account for the high dividend yields provided by the two funds. So while from a fundamental standpoint the two funds are very different, from a total return perspective, international HY and domestic do exhibit very similar traits.

One can consider PGHY as a good portfolio diversifier when it comes to fundamentals (i.e., international companies will have slightly different bankruptcy drivers), despite the close correlations in total returns.

An investor, however, needs to note that in order to turbocharge a HY portfolio, one can also use the VanEck Emerging Markets High Yield Bond ETF (HYEM) fund, a name which we have covered in the past. HYEM covers only true EM credits (i.e., no developed market companies). The fund has outperformed its developed market peers:

During sound fundamental environments, this is to be expected since EM credits need to compensate investors more. If there are no defaults and the macro cycle is benign, EM credits will provide a high total return.

A well-diversified HY portfolio can therefore be composed of JNK, PGHY, and HYEM in order to truly capture all outstanding jurisdictions.

Macro is not helpful - spreads are too tight

So while we like PGHY and what it brings to the table, we are not going to buy it here since the macro conditions are not helpful. In this section we are going to show readers why we believe spreads to be too tight here, thus not making today's price point a good entry into PGHY.

For our analysis we are going to look at a graph of both EM and US HY (US HY as a proxy for developed markets):

The above graph is of the ICE BofA High Yield EM Corporate Plus Index OAS from the Fed. As we can see from the chart, spreads are close to historic lows here. We can also see the quick widening that happened in April 2025, which caused PGHY to have a -5% drawdown.

As a simple rule of thumb, you want to buy EM spreads when they are above 5%, and be very careful when they are at the bottom of their historic range.

Similarly, if we look at US HY as a proxy for developed markets spreads, we see a similar pattern:

U.S. spreads are at historic lows when looking back ten years, and they also widened substantially back in April 2025.

The macro picture is very unhelpful; thus, one should not consider today's market as a good entry point into PGHY, despite its diversification benefits.

Conclusion

PGHY is an exchange-traded fund from Invesco. The name contains a blend of developed markets and EM HY bonds, thus providing for a fundamental diversification brick in a larger portfolio. The fund has a close correlation to its U.S. peer JNK from a total return perspective but provides for fundamental diversification via its holdings. We like PGHY's composition and metrics, but today's macro is not helpful for this name. Credit spreads are very tight internationally; therefore, we are only on 'Hold' with respect to this fund.

data.title: New Mountain Finance: NAV Continues To Slide Through Q3 (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

Business development companies continue to struggle in this higher interest rate environment. The higher cost of debt essentially limits the appeal for businesses to seek debt financing, which stunts growth for BDCs. New Mountain Finance Corp (NMFC) has faced many of the same challenges as the rest of the sector. When I previously covered NMFC, I issued a hold rating due to the lack of earnings growth and NAV decline. Now that NMFC reported its Q3 earnings, I wanted to revisit the performance and outlook for the remainder of 2025.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that NMFC's share price has declined by about 18.5%. NMFC has essentially failed to participate in the growth of the overall market over the last few months. Even when including all distributions that were paid out to investors, the total return still sits at a loss of 8.6% over the same time frame. NMFC now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 13.4%, which makes it an enticing choice for investors seeking income generation. However, I remain cautious about the overall sustainability of payouts in the near term.

Despite NMFC trading at a large discount to NAV valuation, I believe there is continued downside risk. Management has failed to allocate a meaningful level of capital towards new investment activity, which means that there's no indication that the current NAV decline will shift its momentum. Additionally, NMFC's non-accrual rate has experienced an uptick in non-accruals since my last coverage. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying portfolio strategy and earnings.

Q3 Earnings

According to the latest portfolio breakdown, NMFC's investment portfolio has a fair value of $2.95B that is spread across 127 different portfolio companies. In order to generate its earnings, NMFC lends capital to middle market companies and collects interest and equity in return. NMFC primarily leans on first lien debt securities to generate its earnings, which is ideal because first lien debt sits at the top of the corporate capital structure. This form of debt has the highest priority for repayment, which protects NMFC against losing all invested capital in a bad transaction. The full breakdown by debt type is as follows:

  • First Lien Debt: 67%
  • SLPs: 9%
  • Preferred: 7%
  • Common Equity & Other: 5%
  • Net Lease: 4%
  • Subordinated:4%
  • Second Lien Debt: 4%

Expanding on this diversity, NMFC allocates its portfolio across a diverse range of industries and sub-sectors. The bulk of the portfolio is allocated across Software, Healthcare, and Business Service investments. However, management spreads its investments across a ton of sub-sectors that help mitigate any sort of concentration risk.

NMFC reported its Q3 earnings at the start of November and the results were a bit disappointing. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.32 per share, which was in-line with the prior quarters. Total investment income amounted to $80.4M, but this was a decline from the prior quarter's total investment income of $83.4M. The lower income can be attributed to a lesser amount of interest income received during the quarter, which now amounted to $59.4M.

Additionally, NMFC was not able to capture any net realized gains during the quarter. However, the portfolio did see a net change in unrealized depreciation of $21.6M during the quarter. The portfolio position has continued to struggle with appreciating over time, reinforced by twelve straight months of declines.

On a more positive note, NMFC was able to offset these lower earnings by reducing its operating expenses. Management fees saw a slight decline from $9.8M in the prior quarter, to $9.66M in the most recent quarter. Additionally, the incentive fee was nearly cut in half and amounted to $2.8M. However, NMFC did see the amount of interest it's paying on debt rise to $31.7M. Overall, total net expenses declined to $46.5M for the quarter.

From a balance sheet perspective, NMFC has some room for improvement. For instance, the statutory debt to equity ratio has increased to 1.26x, indicating that the portfolio is more reliant on borrowed capital to fuel its growth. Statutory debt for the quarter has increased to $1.58B, up from the prior quarter's total debt of $1.52B. However, NMFC did improve its cash and equivalents, which has now increased to $63.7M.

Valuation & Risk Profile

Since NMFC is structured as a business development company, the price can trade at a difference from the underlying value of its assets. As a result of the continuous decline, NMFC now trades at a large discount to NAV of 20.90%. For reference, NMFC has traded at an average discount to NAV of 5.2% over the last five years. The red line on the chart below clearly indicates that NMFC now trades at one of the largest discounts to NAV levels over the last five years. However, I believe that this discount is a direct representation of the challenges that NMFC continues to face.

Wall Street analysts currently have an average price target of $10 per share, which indicates a small ~4% upside from the current level. While this small upside is certainly possible, I believe the growth potential remains limited. For instance, management was only able to allocate $127.3M towards new investments during the quarter. However, this was offset by repayments totaling $177.1M, which ultimately resulted in a net decline of $49.8M. This is now the fourth consecutive quarter of NMFC experiencing a net decline in new investment activity. This has led to the BDC's NAV trending downward over the last year:

  • Q3 2024: $12.62 per share
  • Q4 2024: $12.55 per share
  • Q1 2025: $12.45 per share
  • Q2 2025: $12.21 per share
  • Q3 2025: $12.06 per share

Additionally, NMFC has seen a slight uptick in its level of non-accruals. Non-accruals represent the amount of portfolio companies that are materially underperforming and cannot keep up with their required debt maintenance. According to the latest earnings call, non-accruals increased to 1.7% of fair portfolio value, indicating that a greater amount of borrowers are struggling to repay their debts. Management confirmed that the uptick can be attributed to weaker consumer demand.

During the quarter, we moved our first lien debt position in Beauty Industry Group to nonaccrual status and expect to equitize a portion of this debt position in the coming months. The company has experienced persistent earnings headwinds due to weaker consumer demand, specific go-to-market challenges and tariffs on its China-oriented supply chain. In coordination with the other lender, we have built a large New Mountain team that will be focused on improving this investment. - John Kline, President

TO NMFC's credit, the non-accrual rate doesn't really sit that much higher than some notable peers in the BDC sector. Despite the Fed initiating its second 25 basis point cut to interest rates, the higher rate environment still limits the amount of businesses seeking debt financing. For reference, here are the non-accrual rates for some peer BDCs:

  • Ares Capital (ARCC): 1% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.
  • Barings BDC Inc (BBDC): 0.4% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.
  • PennantPark Investment (PNNT): 0.7% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.32 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 13.4%. As previously mentioned, net investment income amounted to $0.32 per share for the quarter, which does indicate that earnings currently support the payouts. However, this leaves essentially no margin for error. So if non-accruals increase and net investment income is negatively impacted, NMFC's distributions will be under threat of reduction.

However, it seems that management is confident in the portfolio's ability to continue supporting payouts. This is achieved through the combination of a dividend protection program and recurring income from their loan portfolio. Unfortunately, management didn't confirm any sort of undistributed spillover income that is available.

Our net investment income and dividend were supported by consistent recurring income from our loan portfolio, full utilization of the dividend protection program, which remains in place through the fourth quarter of 2026 and an incremental fee waiver. Looking forward to Q4, we would like to announce a $0.32 dividend payable on December 31 to shareholders of record on December 17. - Steven Klinsky, CEO

Lastly, investors should be aware that the distributions received from NMFC do not offer any tax efficiency. Since payouts are fueled primarily by net investment income, the distributions will be classified as ordinary dividends, which have the greatest tax burden for investors. So if you want to limit the tax burden from all distributions received, a position in NMFC would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am downgrading my rating on NMFC to a sell due to the NAV decline and failure to allocate capital towards new investments during the quarter. Additionally, non-accruals experienced a slight uptick, which is another indication of the challenges that the portfolio faces. If NMFC isn't capable of growing its portfolio through new investments, it is unlikely that the net investment income will grow and the NAV decline will be reversed. So although NMFC can still support its distributions, I believe that there is more downside risk ahead.

data.title: Planet Fitness: Aggressive Growth Targets Through FY 2028
data.description:
Markets are rebounding sharply as we head into year-end, reflecting increased confidence in the prospects for a rate cut and Q3 earnings that largely beat expectations, despite clear signs of weakening consumer spending. It's hardly a time for complacency, however, and with so much of the S&P 500's gains this year being driven by large-cap tech stocks, I think investors should spend effort in the last month of the year ensuring we're not overexposed to the AI trade and rotating toward more "growth at a reasonable price" stocks.

Planet Fitness (PLNT), in my view, is one of these great companies to invest in. Since the start of the year, Planet Fitness has gained ~12%, slightly trailing the S&P 500 despite accelerating growth trends, with enthusiasm driven by price increases in the company's standard membership tier. The company recently reported a healthy beat-and-raise in Q3 that sparked a fresh rally in the name. With a substantial growth opportunity still ahead of it, I think Planet Fitness is still in the earlier stages of a larger rally.

I last wrote a buy article on Planet Fitness in September, when the stock was hovering around $100 per share. Since then, Planet Fitness has initiated new medium-term growth targets that point to an expected acceleration in adjusted EBITDA growth. To me, this is a clear reflection that Planet Fitness is recognizing its pricing power to boost the rates on its still-modestly priced memberships, with little churn recognized to date. As the company is unlocking substantial top- and bottom-line growth, I'm reiterating my buy rating on this company.

Let's first check in on Planet Fitness' valuation multiples against its raised guidance targets before digging into the company's longer-term expectations. At current share prices near $112, Planet Fitness trades at a market cap of $9.33 billion. After we net off the $529.5 million of cash and $2.16 billion of debt on the company's latest balance sheet, Planet Fitness' resulting enterprise value is $10.96 billion.

For the remainder of FY25, Planet Fitness has guided to 11% y/y revenue growth, which is up one point from a previous 10% growth expectation, driven by 50 bps of expected same-club sales growth improvement versus its prior guidance. Management is now also expecting 12% y/y adjusted EBITDA growth (~$546 million in adjusted EBITDA) at a ~41.7% margin.

For FY26, Wall Street is expecting Planet Fitness to generate $1.46 billion in revenue, at an 11% y/y growth pace. If we assume 100 bps of y/y adjusted EBITDA margin expansion to 42.7% margins (flat to Q3 actual margins and roughly in line with the company's current ~1 point of y/y margin expansion), adjusted EBITDA would be $623.4 million (+14% y/y), also in line with the company's plan to grow adjusted EBITDA at a mid-teens pace through the next several years.

This positions Planet Fitness' valuation multiples at:

  • 20.1x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA
  • 17.6x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA

While Planet Fitness isn't exactly a value stock, I consider it to be a "growth at a reasonable price" company when we consider the substantial growth trajectory that the company is expecting through FY28. In a recent mid-November Investor Day, Planet Fitness laid out new targets, expecting a continuation of mid single digit same-club sales growth (driven by the Black Card price increase in 2026), 6-7% annual franchise unit growth, and mid-teens adjusted EBITDA growth.

To me, Planet Fitness is enjoying two secular tailwinds. The first is a clear trend into health and fitness that is driving gym sign-ups; the second is consumers pinching their budgets, which will accentuate Planet Fitness' value appeal (even after raising prices, Planet Fitness' $15 cost for a standard membership, or $30 for a Black Card with extra perks and all-location access, is still at industry lows). Stay long here and keep riding the recent rally higher.

Q3 recap

Let's now go through Planet Fitness' latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Planet Fitness' revenue grew 13% y/y to $330.3 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $323.4 million (+11% y/y) by a two-point margin. Meanwhile, same club sales growth - which as a reminder, measures the company's revenue growth from existing club locations, excluding the impact of newly opened locations - grew at 6.9% y/y, which is a slight deceleration from 8.2% in Q2, but still higher than 6.1% in Q1.

We appreciate the fact that Planet Fitness' growth is evenly distributed between unit expansion and each club generating more revenue. Within the quarter, Planet Fitness added 35 net-new locations, ending Q3 with 2,795 total Planet Fitness gyms.

Management notes that a strong mix shift up from the Classic to the Black Card and strong membership retention triggered the decision to begin raising Black Card membership prices from $25 to $30 next year. With roughly two-thirds of the company's membership on this tier, the ~20% jump in Black Card rates will drive 13% growth in same-club sales alone, excluding any offsetting churn impacts. Per CEO Colleen Keating's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

We also saw increased Black Card penetration in the quarter with 66.1% of our total membership now at the higher tier, a 300 basis point increase from the same quarter last year. Consumers continue to recognize the value of the Black Card with the smallest price gap between our 2 membership tiers since we launched the Black Card.

As many of you know, we held off on increasing the price of our Black Card membership until we got on the other side of the Classic Card price increase anniversary. After thoughtful consideration, significant testing and data analysis, we've made the decision to raise the Black Card price to $29.99 after our peak join season in 2026. We're also continuing to test new Black Card amenities such as dry cold plunge and red light technology that would add even more value to our Black Card offering."

Planet Fitness is also encouraging new unit expansion by rolling out a new, smaller club format, which likely has less upfront capex burden. The company notes that 95% of franchisees who were either developing a new location or renovating an existing one chose the new, optimized gym format.

On the profitability side, Planet Fitness continued to benefit from economies of scale, especially as the company cut selling, general, and administrative expenses by -6% y/y to $30.5 million, despite the double-digit growth in revenue and franchise footprint expansion.

As a result, adjusted EBITDA grew 14% y/y to $140.8 million, reflecting a 42.7% margin that improved 60 bps versus 42.1% in the year-ago Q3.

Key takeaways

Planet Fitness is a terrific business with a base of 20.7 million members, a number that is still growing and captive to planned price increases. The company has an aggressive plan to expand its footprint via 6-7% annual locations growth on top of successful recent price increases. Its mid/high teens adjusted EBITDA multiple is a reasonable valuation for a company planning mid-teens adjusted EBITDA growth through the next few years. Stay long here.

data.title: Producer Price Index: Wholesale Inflation Up 0.3% In September
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The latest report on the Producer Price Index (PPI) shows that wholesale inflation for final demand increased by 0.3% in September. This uptick was in line with expectations and follows a -0.1% decline in August. On an annual basis, headline PPI increased 2.7% year-over-year, unchanged from August and consistent with expectations.

Core PPI, which excludes food and energy prices, was up 0.1% month-over-month after a -0.1% decline in August. This was below the expected 0.2% monthly growth. On an annual basis, core PPI cooled to 2.6% in September from 2.9% in August. This was lower than the expected 2.7% reading and is the smallest annual growth since April 2024.

Producer Price Index: Finished Goods

The BLS shifted its focus to the "final demand" PPI series in 2014, but data for these series extend only back to November 2009 for headline PPI and April 2010 for core PPI. Since our analysis emphasizes longer-term trends, we continue to track the legacy PPI for finished goods, which the BLS still includes in its monthly updates. As a later overlay chart will illustrate, the final demand and finished goods indexes remain highly correlated.

In September, the PPI for finished goods rose 1.2% month-over-month, up from 0.1% in August. Year-over-year, headline PPI for finished goods heated up to 3.3% from 2.0% in August. Meanwhile, core PPI for finished goods was up 0.2% on the month, down from 0.4% in August. On an annual basis, core PPI for finished goods was unchanged at 2.9%.

Producer Price Index (PPI) vs. Consumer Price Index (CPI)

Both PPI and CPI illustrate monthly price changes. However, as their names suggest, the Producer Price Index measures price changes from the producer perspective, whereas the Consumer Price Index measures price changes from the consumer perspective.

PPI is thought to be a leading indicator of consumer inflation because, for the most part, when producers pay more for goods and services, they are likely to pass along those higher costs to the consumer. With that being said, during the 2020 recession, producers were unable to pass along price increases, demonstrating the higher volatility of core PPI than core CPI. This relationship is further illustrated in the next chart.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: VT: Sticking With Global Stocks As The Bull Market Matures
data.description:
International stocks are leading the way in 2025. The Vanguard FTSE All-World ex-US ETF (VEU) has returned 29% through Thanksgiving, outpacing the Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF (VTI) by 12 percentage points. Yes, the US Dollar Index (DXY) is down in 2025, but investors should not dismiss what’s happening globally.

Back in April, I had a buy rating on the Vanguard Total World Stock Market ETF (VT). I reiterate a buy rating today. Its valuation remains decent, while the bull market shows few signs of letting up. What’s more, December through early January has tended to be a strong stretch, particularly after big January through October returns.

Foreign Stocks Beating US Stocks YTD, Dollar -8%

According to the issuer, VT invests in both foreign and U.S. stocks, and it seeks to track the performance of the FTSE Global All Cap Index, which covers both well-established and still-developing markets. The ETF has high-growth potential but also high risk and can serve as a solid long-term, low-cost ETF for diversified investors.

VT is a large ETF with $74 billion in assets under management as of November 26, 2025. That’s up from $54 billion at the time of my previous analysis, while its annual expense ratio is low at just 6 basis points. The fund’s trailing 12-month dividend yield is moderate at 1.67%, which is about 0.4 percentage point higher than that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum has been very strong ever since the April 9 post-Liberation Day low, earning the product a robust B+ ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. In terms of risk, the fund also scores well, thanks to its ample geographic diversification and limited historical realized volatility readings.

On liquidity, VT is highly tradable. Average daily volume is north of 3 million shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is tight at just two basis points, per Vanguard. I like to split up US vs ex-US holdings by account type.

It's not a huge amount of potential tax alpha, but the foreign tax credit can help (particularly for investors with a low marginal tax bracket). The offset is that non-US equity dividends are typically non-qualified (taxed at your income tax rate).

For an update on the portfolio, the 1-star, Gold-rated ETF by Morningstar plots on the top row of the style box, indicating its high bent to global large caps. There’s about 18% access to mid-caps, while small caps make up only 5% of the fund.

As it stands, the price-to-earnings ratio is reasonable at 18.8x. Coupled with a long-term EPS growth rate of 9.3x, and the resulting PEG ratio is 2.0x... not incredibly cheap, but by no means super pricey.

VT: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

As for the sector breakdown, VT is more diverse than the S&P 500. The Information Technology sector commands a material 28% of the fund, though that is about 6 percentage points below the group’s weight in the SPX.

Financials, Industrials, and Consumer Discretionary (three cyclical sectors) are the next largest. As we approach the end of 2025, fully five sectors are less than 5% of the global stock market each.

VT: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, December has actually tended to feature muted returns. And that lackluster trend has, on average, played out through Q1. So, calendar trends are not wholly bullish when analyzing the past 10 years of price data.

VT: Neutral Seasonality Last 10 Years December-March

The Technical Take

With at least a fair valuation, VT’s technical situation is solid. Notice in the chart below that shares remain in a protracted uptrend. I see support near the $132 to $135 range, while resistance may come into effect at the all-time high of $142.

But with a rising long-term 200-day moving average, the bulls clearly control the primary trend. What’s more, a larger support layer is seen down at $124. Based on the depth of the February to April decline and subsequent breakout later in the second quarter, a measured move upside price target to $147 is in play.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It printed a series of lower highs while the price ascended. That’s a yellow flag... a bearish RSI divergence to price. Hence, we could see a continuation of the sideways trend. But I expect an ultimate breakout before long.

VT: Current Consolidation Has Support in the Mid-$130s, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on VT. I see global stocks as fairly priced within a broader bull market. So, stay on board into year-end, with 2026 coming closer into view.
